title: Electronic primary thermometry -- experimental comparison of the Coulomb Blockade and Shot Noise Thermometer
authors: Eemil Praks, Libin Wang, Yu-Cheng Chang, Renan P. Loreto, Mika Prunnila, Joonas T. Peltonen, Jukka P. Pekola
abstract: Since the redefinition of the kelvin in 2019, new methods of primary thermometry have been considered to replace the currently agreed temperature scale in the very high and low temperature limits. These new methods should provide improved uncertainties and, most importantly, a more direct link to the definition of the kelvin. In the low temperature ranges it is possible to realize thermometers that do not require any known reference temperature or external calibration parameters. We present an experimental comparison of two such thermometers: the Coulomb Blockade Thermometer (CBT) and the Shot Noise Thermometer (SNT). Both thermometers measure temperature hinging only on the natural constants $k_\mathrm{B}$, and $e$. Furthermore both of them are based on electron tunneling current and, thereby, need only electrical measurements, enhancing the practicality. CBT and the SNT are inter-compared in a range of 20 mK to 235 mK. The results show that the agreement of SNT and CBT is approximately within 2.5 % in this range. Basic measurement uncertainty is analyzed and we show that uncertainty in the measurement frequency can cause significant error. to temperature measurement of the SNT at low temperatures where finite frequency plays a role.
summary: This paper experimentally compares two primary electronic thermometers, the Coulomb Blockade Thermometer (CBT) and the Shot Noise Thermometer (SNT), for use in the millikelvin regime. By measuring electrical properties like conductance and noise power, the authors demonstrate that both methods yield highly consistent temperature readings. This work provides a robust, fundamental-constant-based tool for precise temperature measurement in low-temperature quantum experiments.
main_problem: The paper addresses the need for primary thermometry methods to replace the current temperature scale, especially in the very high and low temperature limits.
main_result: The Coulomb Blockade Thermometer (CBT) and the Shot Noise Thermometer (SNT) show good agreement (within 2.5%) when inter-compared in the 20 mK to 235 mK range.
method: The authors experimentally compare two independent electronic thermometers, CBT and SNT, by measuring their respective electrical responses (conductance and noise power) as a function of bias voltage.
model_or_system: The study utilizes quantum circuits involving Normal Metal-Insulator-Normal Metal (NIN) tunnel junctions. The CBT relies on the bias dependence of normalized conductance, while the SNT uses the symmetric current noise, both linking temperature directly to fundamental constants ($k_B, e$).
key_observables: Normalized conductance ($G/G_T$) for CBT and symmetric current noise power ($S_I$) for SNT.
important_parameters: Temperature range of 20 mK to 235 mK; fundamental constants $k_B$ and $e$.
main_assumptions: The comparison assumes that the measurements are free from significant self-heating effects and that the SNT measurement uncertainty is dominated by the measurement bandwidth.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the measured CBT conductance vs. bias voltage and SNT noise power vs. bias voltage, showing best-fit curves derived from theoretical models at specific temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the need for primary thermometry, details the theoretical basis and measurement techniques for CBT and SNT, presents the experimental comparison across a temperature range, and concludes by analyzing the agreement and sources of uncertainty.
why_interesting: The development of highly accurate, fundamental-constant-based thermometers is crucial for any experiment requiring precise temperature control in the quantum regime, directly impacting measurements in quantum optics and many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of the operating principles of (a) CBT and (b) SNT. In CBT Coulomb barrier needs to be overcome by thermal fluctuations, whereas in SNT temper- ature determines the cross-over between thermal noise and Shot noise. (c) CBT and SNT are mounted to the same sam- ple holder to minimize the possiblity of thermal gradients be- tween the two thermometers.

Figure 3. (a) Measured SNT noise power and best fit to it at 100 mK cryostat setpoint. (b) Schematic of the SNT measurement setup. The DC biasing and RF components are separated using large inductors and capacitors mounted on the sample holder. Final measurement bandwidth is fully determined by the components on the RF chain due to the wide bandwidth diode in the measurement. Noise outside the bandwidth does not affect the measurement.

Figure 4. (a) Measured SNT and fridge RuOx thermometer temperatures as a function of CBT temperature. The mixing chamber plate RuOx temperatures deviate systemically from both the CBT and SNT. (b) Relative difference between the CBT and SNT. The point where kBT/h = 750 MHz is marked by a dashed vertical line. Below this point, the uncertainty of the SNT measurement increases significantly. At the lowest temperature, slightly above 20 mK, the uncertainty is approx- imately ±15 %.

Figure 5. Measured CBT curves and least squares fits at a variety of temperatures. While the noise of the measurement increases at lower temperatures due to smaller excitation volt- age, the overall uncertainty remains approximately constant due to the deeper conductance dip.

Figure 7. The temperature of two RuOx thermometers mounted to the MXC plate of the cryostat over a range of tem- peratures. In the presented experiments, RuOx 1 was used. There is a nearly constant offset between the two sensors, suggesting a poor calibration or drift due to aging. The ex- periment was repeated with the position of RuOx 2 changed, which resulted in a similar deviation. This implies that the MXC plate is uniformly thermalized.
